metadata.summary: Docker Compose for CI/CD: compose.ci.yaml override with no ports, generous start_period, up --wait for health checks, always teardown.

content.title: Docker Compose (CI/CD)
content.summary: Docker Compose patterns for CI/CD pipelines: compose.ci.yaml override with no port bindings, generous start_period, docker compose up -d --wait for health checks, and if: always() teardown.

why.p: CI runners are slower than dev machines — health check start_period that works locally will time out on shared CI runners. Port bindings in CI cause conflicts when multiple jobs run concurrently on the same runner. --wait eliminates manual sleep/polling. Missing if: always() on teardown causes volumes to accumulate on failed runs, filling the runner disk.

list.item: Spinning up integration test stacks in CI (app + DB + dependencies)
Building application images in CI with docker compose build before pushing to registry
Running database migrations and smoke tests against a freshly composed stack
Parameterizing compose files with override files for local dev vs CI vs production configurations
Production deployments where zero-downtime is required — use Kubernetes rolling updates
Multi-host deployments in CI — use Kubernetes test clusters or Testcontainers
Pipelines needing only a single container — docker run in the pipeline is simpler
Teams with Kubernetes already in production — align CI with prod using helm/kubectl

rule.statement: Create a compose.ci.yaml override file that only changes CI-specific settings. Base the CI stack on the production compose.yaml plus the override: docker compose -f compose.yaml -f compose.ci.yaml up -d --wait
rule.rationale: Keeping a separate CI override avoids duplicating the full service definition. The override only sets what is different in CI: no port bindings, generous timeouts, CI-injected secrets.

rule.statement: Replace ports: mappings with expose: in the CI override for all internal services. Use the service DNS name for inter-container communication inside CI. Port bindings cause failures when multiple CI jobs run concurrently on the same runner.
rule.rationale: ports: maps a host port to a container port. On a shared CI runner with concurrent jobs, two jobs trying to bind the same host port (e.g., 5432 for PostgreSQL) will fail. expose: makes the port available only within the Docker network.

example.code:
# compose.ci.yaml services: db: # Remove: ports: ["5432:5432"] from compose.override.yaml (dev only) expose: ["5432"] # Internal only — no host port binding
app: expose: ["8000"] environment: - DATABASE_URL=postgres://db:5432/app # Use service name, not localhost

rule.statement: Set start_period to at least 120s for database services in the CI override. Shared CI runners have variable CPU allocation — a database that starts in 5s locally may take 60s+ on a shared runner.
rule.rationale: Health check start_period is the grace period before failure counts start. If start_period is too short on a slow runner, the health check fails before the database is ready, causing depends_on: service_healthy to never become true.

rule.statement: In the CI override, inject secrets via environment variables from CI secrets (GitHub Secrets, GitLab CI variables) rather than copying the .env file pattern. Never check .env into CI or copy it to runners.
rule.rationale: The .env file exists on developer machines and should not be replicated to CI. CI systems have their own secret management (GitHub Secrets, GitLab Variables) that injects values as environment variables at run time.

rule.statement: In CI pipelines using Compose for integration tests, always follow this order: (1) docker compose config --quiet to validate, (2) docker compose up -d --wait to start and wait for healthy, (3) run tests, (4) docker compose down -v --remove-orphans in an if: always() step.
rule.rationale: Validation catches YAML errors before spinning up containers. --wait blocks until all health checks pass, eliminating sleep hacks. if: always() on teardown ensures volumes are cleaned up even when tests fail.

rule.statement: Use docker compose up -d --wait (Compose v2.1+) instead of manual sleep or polling loops in CI. --wait blocks until all services report healthy status before returning control to the pipeline.
rule.rationale: Manual sleep is fragile: too short causes test failures on slow runners, too long wastes CI minutes. --wait adapts to actual readiness, blocking exactly as long as needed.

antipattern.description: Using sleep 30 after docker compose up -d in CI to "wait for services to start".
antipattern.reason: Sleep values that work in test become flaky on slower runners and waste time on faster ones. Use --wait with proper health checks instead.

rule.statement: Always run docker compose down -v --remove-orphans in a step with if: always() in GitHub Actions (or equivalent always() in GitLab CI). This ensures cleanup runs whether tests pass or fail.
rule.rationale: If tests fail, the pipeline step is skipped by default. Without if: always() on teardown, volumes accumulate across failed runs and fill the runner disk. --remove-orphans removes containers from previous runs that share the project name.

rule.statement: Configure Docker layer cache in GitHub Actions using buildx with --cache-from and --cache-to type=registry. Without explicit cache configuration, every CI run rebuilds all layers from scratch.
rule.rationale: Docker build cache is not shared between CI runner jobs by default. Registry-mode cache stores layers in the container registry and is accessible across all runners, dramatically reducing build time for subsequent runs.

template.purpose: CI override: no port bindings, generous health timeouts, secrets from env vars
GitHub Actions job steps for compose-based integration tests
